name: Umm Salama
title: Umm Salama (RA): The Separated Family
meta_description: The story of Umm Salama (RA), part one: a noble woman of Makkah who was torn from her husband and child for her faith, made the journey to Madinah alone, and learned the dua for every calamity.
keywords: Umm Salama, story of Umm Salama, Abu Salama, Hind bint Abi Umayya, mothers of the believers, dua for calamity, sahaba stories
lede: There is a woman who walked out of Makkah with only a camel and a baby, heading for a city weeks away, with no one to protect her on the road. She was not running from her family. Her family was the reason she had to go.

This is the first part of the story of Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her): a daughter of one of Makkah's noblest houses, the wife of a man worthy of his own biography, and a mother who held on to her faith while almost everything she loved was pulled away from her.

sections.h2: A daughter of a noble house
paras: Her name was Hind, and she came from Banu Makhzum, one of the most powerful tribes in Makkah. It was the tribe of Abu Jahl. It was the tribe that tortured the family of Yasir. It was also a tribe that would one day give Islam some of its greatest companions, and she was among the first of them.

Her father, Abu Umayya, was not just any man of Makhzum. He was the chief of the tribe, known for his nobility and his generosity, and famous above all for feeding and hosting the pilgrims who came to Makkah. In a city where clans competed over who could be most hospitable, he was the one who paid for it. She grew up in the home of a leader, surrounded by status and comfort.

Her brothers would all become Muslim in time, and one of them accepted Islam only at the conquest of Makkah, years after her. So much of what we know about her comes through a brother narrating from his sister. But she went ahead of every one of them. She believed first.

There is one more thread worth holding. Through the milk that nursed them, she was counted as a sister of Ammar ibn Yasir, whose family Banu Makhzum would torture for their faith. So the same tribe that produced enemies of the Prophet ﷺ also raised people whose hearts would belong to him completely. Lineage decided nothing. Character decided everything.

sections.h2: Abu Salama, and the first migration
paras: Her husband was Abu Salama, Abdullah ibn Abd al-Asad, also of Banu Makhzum. He was a first cousin of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ through his mother, and a milk-brother as well, so the bond between them ran deep even before Islam.

Abu Salama was among the very earliest to believe. Some accounts place him as the eleventh person to accept Islam, before the Muslims even began gathering in secret. Umm Salama followed soon after, counted among the first handful of believers in the world.

When the persecution grew unbearable, Abu Salama and Umm Salama were among the first to leave for Abyssinia, and they are remembered as the first family to make that journey. They migrated there twice. The second time was because a rumour reached them that Makkah had embraced Islam, and when they returned and found it false, they turned back to Abyssinia again.

Almost everything we know about those years across the sea is preserved through her eyes. The stories of the king who protected the Muslims, the two armies, the messengers sent to drag them home: she carried those memories and passed them on. Their son Salama was born in that distant land, in exile. By the time they finally came back to Makkah, this was a family that had already given up home twice for the sake of their faith.

sections.h2: The day the family was torn apart
paras: When word reached them that the Prophet ﷺ was preparing to migrate to Madinah, the small family packed their belongings and set out together: Abu Salama, Umm Salama, and their child. They did not get far.

Her own relatives from Banu Makhzum intercepted them. Abu Salama, they said, could go where he pleased. But Umm Salama was the daughter of their chief, the cousin of Abu Jahl, one of their own, and she would not be leaving with him. They forced the two apart and sent Abu Salama on the road to Madinah alone.

Then the family of Abu Salama heard what had happened, and their pride was wounded. The child, they argued, belonged to the father's line. They came and pulled the baby from his mother's arms, and in the struggle they wrenched his small shoulder. In a single day she lost her husband and her son, and the men who did it were not strangers but kin.

sections.h2: A year at the Kaaba, and a dua
paras: What followed was not a few weeks. For an entire year she would walk out toward the Kaaba and weep. She would retell what had been done to her, the husband taken, the child taken, and she would turn to Allah and ask for a way out.

Picture the patience that takes. A year of waking each morning to an empty home, carrying the same grief to the same place, and refusing to let it harden into despair. Eventually her husband's clan returned her son to her, and in time even her own relatives softened. What harm would it do, some of them said, to let the poor woman go to her husband? At last she was told she was free.
She had been afflicted with exactly the kind of loss the Qur'an speaks of, and she met it the way the steadfast are described as meeting it:

ayah.text: We shall certainly test you with fear and hunger, and loss of property, lives, and crops. But [Prophet], give good news to those who are steadfast, those who say, when afflicted with a calamity, 'We belong to God and to Him we shall return.'

paras: She did not wait for an escort. She mounted her camel, set her baby in front of her, and started for Madinah by herself. It was a journey of weeks, and a dangerous one. She could have been robbed. She could have been lost. Her child could have died on the way. She told it simply: I was not thinking about any of that. I only wanted my family back.

A few miles out, at a place called Tan'im, she met a man named Uthman ibn Talha. He was not a Muslim. He was a man of high standing, the keeper of the keys of the Kaaba. He saw a woman alone with a baby on the open road and asked where she was going. To my husband in Madinah, she said. And who is with you? No one, she answered, no one but Allah and this child.

By Allah, he said, you will not be left to do this alone. What kind of man would let you? He took the camel's rein and walked. He never rode, because there was nothing for him to ride: he made the whole journey on foot. At every stop he would set the camel down, walk far away, and turn his back so she could rest in privacy. Then he would bring it close, hold it steady, and walk on. She said she had never met a more honourable man among the Arabs.

When they reached the outskirts of Madinah, Uthman pointed to the settlement where her husband was staying. Your husband is there, he said. May Allah be with you. Then he turned around and walked all the way back to Makkah, on foot, the way he had come.

He did not go to Abu Salama to be paid. He did not ask for a camel or a coin for two long journeys on his own legs. He had done it out of pure decency, for a woman who was not his relative, on behalf of a faith he did not yet hold. Set that against the men who tore the family apart out of arrogance and ego, and the contrast tells you everything.

Years later Uthman ibn Talha would accept Islam, and at the conquest of Makkah the Prophet ﷺ placed the keys of the Kaaba back in his hands and ordered that they remain with his descendants forever. The man who asked for nothing was repaid with an honour that has passed down his line to this day.

sections.h2: A loss, and a promise kept
paras: In Madinah the family was whole again, and more children followed. Abu Salama fought at Badr and at Uhud, where he was wounded. He came home one day full of joy and told Umm Salama of something he had heard from the Prophet ﷺ: whoever says, at the moment of calamity, that to Allah we belong and to Him we return, and asks Allah to reward him in his loss and replace it with something better, will be given something better. He did not know he was teaching his wife the very words she would need at his own death.

His wounds from Uhud reopened, and months later he passed away. As she watched him go, she remembered the dua he had carried home to her. She said it, and then a thought came: who could possibly be better than Abu Salama? Yet she had been promised better, and Allah kept the promise. He gave her the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself.

It did not happen at once. She was a widow with young children and a heart heavy with all she had endured. When the proposal came, she answered honestly: I am a jealous woman, I am no longer young, and I have children to raise. The Prophet ﷺ replied that he would ask Allah to ease her jealousy, that he too was advanced in age, and that her children would be his own. Her family, he said, would be his family. And so they were.

lessons_heading: What Umm Salama's story teaches us
lessons_intro: Part of her life is loud with loss, and part of it is quiet with patience. Both halves are worth carrying.

lessons.bold: Faith can cost you the people you love.
lessons.text: She did not lose her family to strangers but to her own kin, and she held on anyway. Conviction is sometimes tested closest to home.

lessons.bold: Patience is a long road, not a moment.
lessons.text: She wept at the Kaaba for a full year before relief came. Steadiness over months, not a single brave act, is what carried her through.

lessons.bold: The dua of calamity is a gift, not a formality.
lessons.text: To Allah we belong, and to Him we return. She said it through real grief, certain that what was taken could be replaced with something better.

lessons.bold: Good character needs no audience and no reward.
lessons.text: Uthman ibn Talha walked weeks on foot for a stranger and asked for nothing. Decency that expects no payment is its own kind of greatness.

faq.a: Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her), whose given name was Hind bint Abi Umayya, was a noblewoman of the Makhzum tribe of Makkah and one of the earliest Muslims. After the death of her husband Abu Salama, she became one of the Mothers of the Believers.

Abu Salama, Abdullah ibn Abd al-Asad, was her first husband: a cousin and milk-brother of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, one of the earliest believers, a man who made the migration to Abyssinia and then to Madinah, and a veteran of Badr. He died from wounds received at Uhud.

faq.q: Why was Umm Salama separated from her family?
faq.a: When the family set out to migrate to Madinah, her own tribe refused to let her leave and forced her apart from Abu Salama. His clan then took their son. She was kept from both for about a year before she was finally allowed to go.

faq.q: Who helped Umm Salama reach Madinah?
faq.a: A man named Uthman ibn Talha, the non-Muslim keeper of the keys of the Kaaba, found her travelling alone with her child. He escorted her the entire way on foot, guarded her dignity at every stop, and returned to Makkah without asking for anything in return.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be pulled apart from our families by a hostile tribe. But most of us know what it is to carry a loss we did not choose, and to wonder whether relief will ever come. Umm Salama's answer was not to pretend the pain away. It was to weep honestly, to keep turning toward Allah, and to trust that what was taken could be returned in a better form.

Her story leaves us with two things worth wanting. The patience to wait without despairing, and the quiet decency of a man who helped a stranger and asked for nothing. Carry one of those into your day, into the next loss or the next chance to help, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umm Salama (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), part one. The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 2:155-156). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

A city weeks away. No one to protect her on the road.

Her name was Hind, daughter of the chief of Banu Makhzum.

Then, as they tried to reach Madinah, her own tribe tore them apart.

Then his clan came for the baby and pulled him from her arms.

In one day she lost them both.

For a whole year she wept at the Kaaba and asked Allah for a way out.

Not a moment of patience. A year of it.

When she was finally freed, she did not wait for an escort.

She set her baby on the camel and started the journey alone.

A man named Uthman ibn Talha found her on the road. He was not even Muslim.

He walked her all the way to Madinah on foot, guarded her dignity, and asked for nothing.

**To Allah we belong, and to Him we return.**

She learned the dua of calamity, and lived it.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her) was a daughter of one of Makkah's noblest houses. When her family tried to migrate for their faith, her own tribe forced her apart from her husband, and his clan took their child. She lost both in a single day.

For a year she wept at the Kaaba and asked Allah for relief. When it finally came, she made the long journey to Madinah alone, with only her baby, until a noble stranger insisted on walking the whole way beside her. Later she would learn the dua for every calamity from the husband she would one day lose, and be given something better.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/umm-salama-part-1

email.subject: She made the journey to Madinah with only a baby and a camel
email.alt_subject: The woman who lost everything in a single day
email.preview: Her own family tore her household apart. A year later, she walked out of Makkah alone.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a woman who once walked out of Makkah with nothing but a camel and a baby, heading for a city weeks away, with no one to protect her on the road.

Her name was Hind, though we know her as Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her). She came from one of the noblest houses of Makkah, the same powerful tribe as Abu Jahl, and she believed in Islam before any of her brothers did.

When she and her husband Abu Salama tried to migrate to Madinah for their faith, her own relatives stopped them. They forced her husband onto the road alone. Then his clan came and pulled their baby from her arms. In a single day she lost both her husband and her son, and the people who did it were not strangers. They were family.

For an entire year she walked out to the Kaaba and wept, and asked Allah for a way out. Not a moment of patience: a year of it. When she was finally allowed to leave, she did not wait for anyone to escort her. She set her baby on her camel and started the journey alone.

A few miles out she met a man named Uthman ibn Talha, the keeper of the keys of the Kaaba. He was not even a Muslim. When he saw her travelling alone, he said, by Allah, you will not be left to do this alone. He walked her the entire way to Madinah on foot, guarded her dignity at every stop, and when they arrived he turned around and walked back to Makkah without asking for anything in return.

In Madinah her family was whole again. There her husband taught her something he had heard from the Prophet ﷺ: that whoever meets calamity by saying that to Allah we belong and to Him we return, and asks Allah for something better, will be given something better. He did not know he was teaching her the very words she would need at his own death.

When he passed away from his wounds, she said those words, even as she wondered who could ever be better than him. And Allah answered her.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: There is a woman who once walked out of Makkah with only a camel and a baby, heading for a city weeks away, with no one to protect her on the road. She was not running from her family. Her family was the reason she had to go.

Her name was Hind, daughter of the chief of Banu Makhzum, but we know her as Umm Salama (may Allah be pleased with her). She came from status and comfort, the same tribe as Abu Jahl, and she believed in Islam before any of her brothers did. Her husband, Abu Salama, was among the very first to believe, and together they were among the first family ever to migrate for their faith.

When word came that the Prophet ﷺ was leaving for Madinah, they packed their belongings and set out as a family. They did not get far. Her own relatives stopped them and forced her husband onto the road alone. Then his clan came and pulled their baby from her arms. In a single day she lost both, and the people who did it were her own kin.

For an entire year she walked out to the Kaaba and wept, and turned to Allah, and asked Him for a way out. It was not a moment of patience. It was a year of it. At last her son was returned to her, and her family relented, and she was told she was free to go.

She did not wait for an escort. She set her baby on the camel and started for Madinah alone. A few miles out she met a man named Uthman ibn Talha, the keeper of the keys of the Kaaba, who was not even a Muslim. By Allah, he said, you will not be left to do this alone. He walked the whole way on foot, turned his back at every stop so she could rest in privacy, and asked for nothing. At the edge of Madinah he pointed to her husband's home, then turned and walked back to Makkah.

In Madinah the family was whole again. There Abu Salama taught her a dua he had heard from the Prophet ﷺ: that whoever meets calamity by saying that to Allah we belong and to Him we return, and asks for something better, will be given it. He did not know he was teaching her the words she would say at his own death.
When his wounds from Uhud took him, she said the dua, even as she wondered who could ever be better than him. And Allah gave her better. But that is the second part of her story.
storybook.pullquote: No one is with me but Allah, and this child of mine.
storybook.reflection: Umm Salama's patience was not a single brave moment. It was a year of weeping at the Kaaba and refusing to let grief turn into despair. Her story asks us to meet our own losses the way she met hers: honestly, without pretending the pain away, and still trusting that what is taken from us can be returned in a better form.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts, part one.
